attributes.stem: An otherwise well 57-year-old man presents with a four hour history of severe right-sided loin pain, radiating into his groin. On examination, his temperature is 37.2C, blood pressure 130/86 mmHg and pulse rate 100/min. His abdomen is soft with no localised tenderness. There is no flank tenderness. Red cells are identified on urinalysis and a non-contrast CT shows a 5mm stone in the distal ureter, with no evidence of hydronephrosis. His haematological and biochemical tests are all normal.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management?
options: Admit for observation until the stone has passed
Analgesia, medical expulsive therapy and observation
Ureteroscopy and stone extraction by basket
attributes.explanation:
This stone is relatively small and should pass of its own accord. It is estimated that most stones 5 mm or less will pass spontaneously and the patient can be treated conservatively whilst the stone passes. In the mean time, the patient should receive appropriate analgesia, such as with regular paracetamol, NSAIDs and prn opioids, and medical expulsive therapy, such as with alpha blockers or calcium channel blockers, unless otherwise contraindicated.

Active intervention should be considered if any of the following apply:

  • Evidence of obstruction

  • Evidence of infection

  • Failure of the stone to pass after a period of medical expulsion therapy

  • Stones ≥10mm and/or proximal ureteral stones (less likely to pass spontaneously)

  • Single kidneys and/or transplanted kidneys with obstruction

  • Bilateral obstruction

  • Intractable pain

If he were septic, urgent decompression is required: if the stone is proximal, nephrostomy is preferred whilst if distal, stent is the intervention of choice.

If the stone is 10-20mm and proximal, lithotripsy is preferred whilst if distal, ureteroscopic fragmentation and extraction with stent is preferred.

If the stone is ≥20mm and/or a staghorn calculi, percutaneous nephrolithotomy is preferred.
learningPoints: Most ureteric stones < 5 mm in size will usually pass spontaneously.
references: Curhan, G, Aronson, M & Preminger, G 2020, Diagnosis and acute management of suspected nephrolithiasis in adults, UpToDate, viewed 10 October 2020, <https://www-uptodate-com.proxy.library.adelaide.edu.au/contents/diagnosis-and-acute-management-of-suspected-nephrolithiasis-in-adults?search=nephrolithiasis&source=searchresult&selectedTitle=1~150&usagetype=default&display_rank=1#H17>.
Miller, NL & Lingeman, JE 2007, ‘Management of kidney stones’, BMJ, vol. 334, no. 7591, pp. 468–472, viewed 10 October 2020, <https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC1808123/>.

Preminger, G 2020, Options in the management of kidney and ureteral stones in adults, UpToDate, viewed 10 October 2020, <https://www-uptodate-com.proxy.library.adelaide.edu.au/contents/options-in-the-management-of-kidney-and-ureteral-stones-in-adults?search=nephrolithiasis&source=searchresult&selectedTitle=2~150&usagetype=default&display_rank=2#H5286840>.
